summary: Lineage land registry — a cadastral record on 164 all-white cords listing which father (tata/papa) owns which terrain (pata) under which administrator (wapa), with the maternal uncle (kaka) as guarantor. Ultra-concentrated vocabulary (6 roots). Same closing signature as UR055: wapa-1OBJ / 1OBJ-chapa ('the administrator is me / my guardian'). Coverage 79.1%.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  164 cords, 163 STRING (99%), 1 INT (value=5), 0 EMPTY, 1 cluster.
  ALL WHITE (W) — 163/163 cords. Unique in the corpus.
  Private Collection: Anna Maria Soldi.
  Urton notes: 'Knots are extremely difficult to read. Cord twist
  approx. 70 deg. Much looser than usual on khipu.'
  Coverage: 79.1% (129/163 cords confirmed).

PHYSICAL CHARACTERISTICS
  All-white khipu with abnormally loose twist (70° vs ~45° normal).
  White = data/neutral color. No chromatic hierarchy — the structure
  IS the data. Made to be read slowly, knot by knot, by someone who
  already knew the system. A spreadsheet, not a narrative.

VOCABULARY — ULTRA-CONCENTRATED (6 roots)
  tata (father, formal/title): x27 — the father of title/rank
  pata (terrain/plot): x18 — the land being registered
  papa (father, kinship): x17 — the father of blood
  wapa (administrator): x14 — the official managing the land
  taka (to hit/stake): x10 — planting boundary markers
  kaka (maternal uncle): x4 — the guarantor/witness
  wata (year): x3 — temporal reference
  paka (hidden/secret): x2 — a contested element
  Additional single occurrences: waka (huaca), pama (formula),
  kama (to govern), wayta (flower), chata (to hit [AY]), chapa (guardian).

  Two words for 'father': tata (respectful/title = fathers of rank)
  vs papa (familiar/kinship = fathers of blood). The register
  distinguishes title-holders from biological parents.

THE STRUCTURE: LINEAGE LAND REGISTRY

  I. GENEALOGICAL OPENING (cords 1-10)
    Seven consecutive fathers (tata/papa) in various grammatical forms:
    PASS-tata (the designated father), tatata (father[ACC]),
    papapa (father[GEN]), tata-GEN, tatalla (father ONLY, limitative).
    Then tata-1OBJ — MY father. Personal claim.
    Then wapapa (of the administrator) and kaka-ACC (the uncle[object]).
    Pattern: paternal lineage → administrative link → maternal lineage.
    The three pillars of Inca succession.

  II. THE BOUNDARY MARKING (cords 11-43)
    taka tata — to stake the father's [boundary]. Taka in cadastral
    context = driving in a boundary marker, not violence.
    Alternating triplets: terrain (pata) + administrator (wapa) +
    path (way) + uncle (kaka) — the elements of a land survey.
    Each stake (taka) linked to a father — each plot bears an
    ancestor's name.
    Cord 36: tata waka — the father of the huaca. Sacred land.
    Cord 38: pata pama — terrain + formula. The ritual words
    pronounced during boundary marking.

  III. THE CENTRAL REGISTER (cords 44-64)
    Regular blocks of three: father — terrain — administrator.
    This is a TABLE: each triplet = one plot assigned to one lineage.
    The white khipu is a spreadsheet — no color because structure
    IS the data. Each group of ~3 cords = one parcel assigned to
    one lineage under one administrator.
    Cord 63: kaka-GEN — of the maternal uncle. The uncle reappears
    as guarantor — Inca land transactions required maternal-line
    witness.

  IV. THE NUMBER AND THE VIOLENCE (cords 65-78)
    Cord 65: NUMBER 5. The only number in the entire khipu.
    Five parcels? Five lineages? Five generations?
    Then the sequence hardens:
    taka-ACC + ACC-papa + 1OBJ-chata + PASS-tata.
    Chata (to hit, Aymara) + taka (to hit, Quechua) — two languages
    for the same act. Bilingual violence or bilingual surveying.
    pata-DIM (cord 78) — the small terrain (diminutive: modest or
    beloved plot).

  V. THE CONTESTED INHERITANCE (cords 79-110)
    pata pata (cord 84) — terrain terrain. Repetition = insistence
    or two adjacent plots.
    tata-GEN / kaka-1OBJ / ACC-wapa — the succession triangle:
    the father who designated, the uncle who witnesses, the
    administrator who manages.
    Chains of administrators: GEN-wapa / papata / ACC-papa / GEN-wapa.
    taka-1OBJ (cord 99) — to stake MINE. Personal boundary.
    papa-DIM (cord 104) — the small/dear father.
    wata-GEN (cord 108) — of the year. Temporal anchor.
    kakaka-GEN (cord 110) — the uncle in full grammatical form.

  VI. THE SECRET AND CLOSING (cords 111-164)
    pata-1OBJ / wapa-1OBJ (cords 116-117) — MY terrain / MY
    administrator. Double personal claim: the land and the charge.
    paka-ACC (cord 124) — the secret[object]. As in UR055, a secret
    element in the succession.
    wayta (cord 134) — the flower. Single occurrence. An offering
    or the beauty of the land, alone among fathers and terrains.
    kama-ACC-GEN (cord 146) — of the act of governing. The word
    kama (to create/govern) appears once, as a seal.
    PASS-paka (cord 149) — the hidden[passive]. Something has been
    hidden. The same secret theme as UR055.
    The two final cords, identical signature to UR055:
      163: wapa-1OBJ — the administrator, ME.
      164: 1OBJ-chapa — MY guardian.

COMPARISON WITH UR055
  UR050 (white) = the REGISTER of lands (cadastral proof)
  UR055 (black) = the ORACLE confirming the right (sacred legitimacy)
  Both share: wapa (administrator), paka (secret), taka (marking/striking),
  papa/tata (father), pata (terrain), kaka (uncle), and the closing
  signature wapa-1OBJ + 1OBJ-chapa.
  White for the cadastre, black for the oracle. Two faces of the same
  power: material proof (UR050) and sacred confirmation (UR055).
  The same administrator may have carried both.

NARRATIVE — THE WHITE REGISTER

  The khipu is white. All of it — one hundred and sixty-three cords,
  all white, all the same. No color to guide the reader, no chromatic
  signal. Just knots on white string, tied loose, seventy degrees
  instead of forty-five. A document meant for someone who already
  knows how to read it.

  It begins with fathers. Seven of them, cord after cord: the father
  who was designated, the father as object of record, the father
  whose land this is, my father, only my father. Then the
  administrator. Then the maternal uncle — the guarantor, the witness
  from the mother's line, as Inca law required.

  What follows is a cadastre. Each group of cords records a triplet:
  which father, which terrain, which administrator. The act of taka
  — staking a boundary — punctuates the list. Ten times the surveyor
  drives his marker into the ground and names the ancestor who owns
  the plot. The uncle watches. The administrator records.

  The terrain of the father. The terrain of the administrator. The
  terrain, the terrain. Plot after plot, stake after stake, father
  after father. A white spreadsheet of land and lineage.

  In the middle, a single number: five. Five parcels, or five
  generations of holders, or five witnesses. The khipu does not
  explain. It counts.

  Then something shifts. Chata — to strike, in Aymara, not Quechua.
  A foreign word for violence, or for a different surveying tradition.
  The secret appears: paka, twice, as in the oracle khipu UR055.
  Something is hidden in the succession. Someone contests.

  But the register continues, implacable. Father, terrain,
  administrator. Father, terrain, administrator. A flower appears
  once — wayta, cord 134 — and vanishes. Kama, the supreme word
  for governance, appears once and seals the record.

  The last two cords say what they always say:
  The administrator is me. The huaca is my guardian.

  This white khipu is the material proof. Somewhere, perhaps in the
  same bundle, a black khipu (like UR055) carried the sacred
  confirmation. The white register proves the land belongs to the
  lineage. The black oracle proves the gods agree. Together, they
  are a title deed — five hundred years old, written in knots.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Locke Word Splitting & suffix attachment
Inca land tenure and kuraka succession: Rostworowski 1999, Historia del Tahuantinsuyu
Role of maternal uncle (kaka) in Inca succession: Zuidema 1990, Inca Civilization in Cuzco
Boundary marking (taka/mojón) in Andean cadastral practice: Murra 1980, Economic Organization of the Inca State
Compare UR055 (succession oracle, same closing signature)
